analogy: Think of "Print Culture and the Modern World" through the chapter's own opening images: SECTION III EVERYDAY LIFE, CULTURE AND POLITICS 105 Print Culture It is difficult for us to imagine a world without printed matter. We find evidence of print everywhere around us – in books, journals, newspapers, prints of famous paintings, and also in everyday things like... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: SECTION III, EVERYDAY LIFE, CULTURE AND POLITICS, 1 The First Printed Books, 1.1 Print in Japan. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Print Culture and the Modern World is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that sECTION III EVERYDAY LIFE, CULTURE AND POLITICS

The text explains that 105 Print Culture It is difficult for us to imagine a world without printed matter.

The text explains that we find evidence of print everywhere around us – in books, journals, newspapers, prints of famous paintings, and also in everyday things like theatre programmes, official circulars, calendars, diaries, advertisements, cinema posters at street corners.

The text explains that we read printed literature, see printed images, follow the news through newspapers, and track public debates that appear in print.

The text explains that we take for granted this world of print and often forget that there was a time before print.

The text explains that we may not realise that print itself has a history which has, in fact, shaped our contemporary world.

The text explains that when did printed literature begin to circulate?

The text explains that in this chapter we will look at the development of print, from its beginnings in East Asia to its expansion in Europe and in India.

The text explains that we will understand the impact of the spread of technology and consider how social lives and cultures changed with the coming of print.

The text explains that p r i n t C u l t u r e a n d t h e M o d e r n W o r l d Chapter V Print Culture and the Modern World Fig.

The text explains that 1 – Book making before the age of print, from Akhlaq-i-Nasiri, 1595.

The text explains that this is a royal workshop in the sixteenth century, much before printing began in India.

The text explains that you can see the text being dictated, written and illustrated.

The text explains that the art of writing and illustrating by hand was important in the age before print.

The text explains that think about what happened to these forms of art with the coming of printing machines.

The text explains that india and the Contemporary World 106 1 The First Printed Books Fig.

The text explains that china possessed a huge bureaucratic system which recruited its personnel through civil service examinations.

The text explains that in the process, the original drawing would be destroyed and only prints would survive.

The text explains that it contains the essential features of Zen Buddhism.

The text explains that in the foreground on the right, compositors are at work, while on the left galleys are being prepared and ink is being applied on the metal types; in the background, the printers are turning the screws of the press, and near them proofreaders are at work.

The text explains that right in front is the final product – the double-page printed sheets, stacked in neat piles, waiting to be bound.

The text explains that as you will see in Chapter 8, they did not read a book individually and silently.

The text explains that on two sides of the goddess, blessing the machine, are Minerva (the goddess of wisdom) and Mercury (the messenger god, also symbolising reason).

The text explains that the Roman Church, troubled by such effects of popular readings and questionings of faith, imposed severe controls over publishers and booksellers and began to maintain an Index of Prohibited Books from 1558.

The text explains that in medieval times, heresy was seen as a threat to the right of the Church to decide on what should be believed and what should not.

The text explains that heretical beliefs were severely punished Satiety – The state of being fulfilled much beyond the point of satisfaction Seditious – Action, speech or writing that is seen as opposing the government Fear of the book Erasmus, a Latin scholar and a Catholic reformer, who criticised the excesses of Catholicism but kept his distance from Luther, expressed a deep anxiety about printing.

The text explains that it may be that one here and there contributes something worth knowing, but the very multitude of them is hurtful to scholarship, because it creates a glut, and even in good things satiety is most harmful ... [printers] fill the world with books, not just trifling things (such as I write, perhaps), but stupid, ignorant, slanderous, scandalous, raving, irreligious and seditious books, and the number of them is such that even the valuable publications lose their value.’ Source Source A Fig.

The text explains that the interior of the printer’s workshop here is the site of a dance of death.

The text explains that thus their ideas about science, reason and rationality found their way into popular literature.

The text explains that if John goes to town with a load of hay, he is charged to be sure not to forget to bring home Peregrine Pickle’s Adventure … and when Dolly is sent to sell her eggs, she is commissioned to purchase The History of Joseph Andrews.’

The text explains that many believed that books could change the world, liberate society from despotism and tyranny, and herald a time when reason and intellect would rule.

The text explains that they devour books, are lost in the world books create, and become enlightened in the process.

The text explains that they argued for the rule of reason rather than custom, and demanded that everything be judged through the application of reason and rationality.

The text explains that the writings of Voltaire and Rousseau were read widely; and those who read these books saw the world through new eyes, eyes that were questioning, critical and rational.

The text explains that all values, norms and institutions were re-evaluated and discussed by a public that had become aware of the power of reason, and recognised the need to question existing ideas and beliefs.

The text explains that in the process, it raised This is how Mercier describes the impact of the printed word, and the power of reading in one of his books: ‘Anyone who had seen me reading would have compared me to a man dying of thirst who was gulping down some fresh, pure water … Lighting my lamp with extraordinary caution, I threw myself hungrily into the reading.

The text explains that source Source B New words Despotism – A system of governance in which absolute power is exercised by an individual, unregulated by legal and constitutional checks

The text explains that discuss questions about the existing social order.

The text explains that they were not influenced directly by everything they read or saw.

The text explains that print did not directly shape their minds, but it did open up the possibility of thinking differently.

The text explains that penny Magazine was published between 1832 and 1835 in England by the Society for the Diffusion of Useful Knowledge.

The text explains that enraged by this, Governor-General Warren Hastings persecuted Hickey, and encouraged the publication of officially sanctioned newspapers that could counter the flow of information that damaged the image of the colonial government.

The text explains that different groups confronted the changes happening within colonial society in different ways, and offered a variety of new interpretations of the beliefs of different religions.

The text explains that the Deoband Seminary, founded in 1867, published thousands upon thousands of fatwas telling Muslim readers how to conduct themselves in their everyday lives, and explaining the meanings of Islamic doctrines.

The text explains that it will be open for free discussion on subjects of general utility, scientific investigation and the speculations connected with the antiquities, statistics, curiosities, history and geography of the country and of the Deccan especially… the patronage and support of all interested in the diffusion of knowledge and Welfare of the People is earnestly solicited.’ Bombay Telegraph and Courier, 6 January 1849 ‘The task of the native newspapers and political associations is identical to the role of the Opposition in the House of Commons in Parliament in England.

The text explains that that is of critically examining government policy to suggest improvements, by removing those parts that will not be to the benefit of the people, and also by ensuring speedy implementation.

The text explains that these prints began shaping popular ideas about modernity and tradition, religion and politics, and society and culture.

The text explains that many journals began carrying writings by women, and explained why women should be educated.

The text explains that a woman in a Tamil novel expressed what reading meant to women who were so greatly confined by social regulations: ‘For various reasons, my world is small … More than half my life’s happiness has come from books …’ While Urdu, Tamil, Bengali and Marathi print culture had developed early, Hindi printing began seriously only from the 1870s.

The text explains that why did some people in eighteenth century Europe think that print culture would bring enlightenment and end despotism?

The text explains that why did some people fear the effect of easily available printed books?

The text explains that choose one example from Europe and one from India.

Source: NCERT Class 10 India and the Contemporary World II, Chapter 5: Print Culture and the Modern World

Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
